title: SELinux context relabelling fails on virtiofs device
body:
### Issue Description

Rootless Podman containers fail to write new files/folders in volumes mounted from virtiofs device - SELinux labels do not get correctly applied resulting in denials. Any files/folders that are already present inside the volume at time of container launch can be accessed and modified as Podman applies the labels at launch, but not during operation.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. On a VM running Podman, mount a virtiofs device
2. Create a folder on the virtiofs device and an empty file
3. Launch a shell container in interactive mode with the folder mounted as a volume
4. Open the empty file and write to it
5. Create a new file on the volume

### Describe the results you received

Describe the results you received

Editing the existing file works as expected
Attempting to create a new file results in Permission denied, and within the container the file appears to not be created
Exiting the container reveals the new file was created as an empty file but not labelled with SELinux context

### Describe the results you expected

Describe the results you expected

Podman applies SELinux labels to new files as they are created to allow containers to write to them

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

The container host is a CoreOS VM running inside Proxmox, with extended attribute remapping enabled using a script as described in the virtiofs documentation (`-xattrmap=":map::user.virtiofs.:"`). As noted, SELinux labelling does work on container launch, just not while the container is running.

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: What AVCs are you seeing?  This seems like a directory is getting created with the wrong label, but without the AVCs I can only guess at the labeling issue.

There's really not much to go on, this is what I get attempting to run "touch test" from a bash container within a volume mounted as /test to the container (AVC pulled from the host of course):

```type=AVC msg=audit(1750752307.335:487): avc:  denied  { read write open } for  pid=2666 comm="touch" path="/test/test" dev="virtiofs" ino=33408 scontext=system_u:system_r:container_t:s0:c558,c660 tcontext=system_u:object_r:unlabeled_t:s0 tclass=file permissive=0```

Basically seems like Podman isn't bothering to label the new file as it gets created. If I run the same container again the denial doesn't happen because, on launch, Podman re-labels everything in the volume to the correct label, it only happens with new files created after the container gets launched.

~~After beating my head against a wall a bit more trying to find a workaround I think this is some sort of interaction between SELinux policies and Podman. I was trying to set the context for the directory using semanage fcontext and still couldn't get it to work, then realised there's an extra built in policy for the /mnt path I was mounting things under that tries to switch off SELinux labelling for subdirectories - I don't know how Podman's SELinux tagging works under the hood but it's acting like it runs chcon on the target directory on launch but seems to rely on some other method to set new files (does chcon set a default context for subdirectories somehow?) and SELinux was reading that policy and not applying labels to new files automatically as a result. Creating a new /var/virtiofs file and setting a default container_file_t context on it works correctly.~~

I'm not sure if this would still be considered at least in part an issue with Podman - as far as I'm aware as long as Podman is allowed to change SELinux labels the entire point of :z/:Z is that the labels should be set to whatever they need to be to give that container access either alone or shared including for new files, but at least there's a way to get it working manually.

EDIT: Still not working properly even with the workaround - not entirely sure why, when I set the file_context.local entry it initially worked but now it isn't, new files are no longer created with the relevant label and Podman containers fail to create new files as a result. This seems to be more of an SELinux issue since new files created directly also don't get labels applied to them, will leave it as is for you guys to call if this is out of scope for Podman

EDIT2: Turns out to be an issue with virtiofsd - for some reason it was letting SELinux labels work for any situation where a file already exists but throwing errors if it was a new file. For reference, despite the documentation for virtiofsd implying otherwise, it turns out you need `--security-label` set even if you are mapping extended attributes to a user key on the host exactly as described in the docs, with the security label flag added it now seems to be working
